title: The process of learning to take up space after shrinking for years
author: Kalesh
excerpt: I spent years making myself small. I don't mean physically small, though there was some of that too. I mean I learned to disappear. To shrink my voice, my need...
metaDescription: I spent years making myself small. I don't mean physically small, though there was some of that too. I mean I learned to disappear. To shrink my voice, my...
htmlContent:
I spent years making myself small. I don't mean physically small, though there was some of that too. I mean I learned to disappear. To shrink my voice, my needs, my presence. I learned that taking up space was dangerous. That being seen meant being hurt. That wanting anything was a setup for disappointment.

Maybe you know what I'm talking about. Maybe you've been doing it so long you forgot there was ever a version of you who took up space without thinking about it. Who laughed loud. Who asked for what they needed. Who let their body be big and visible and real.

Here's the thing. That version of you is still there. They're not gone. They're just buried under years of conditioning, survival strategies, and protective beliefs that kept you safe when you were younger. And learning to take up space again? It's not about becoming someone new. It's about coming back to someone you already are.

The body remembers what the mind forgets

Stephen Porges, the researcher who developed Polyvagal Theory, talks about something called neuroception. It's the way your nervous system scans for safety and danger without you even knowing it's happening. Your body is constantly reading cues. The tone of someone's voice. The way they stand. The space between you and them. And if your nervous system learned, early on, that being seen or heard or big meant danger, then it will keep you small automatically.

That's not a choice. It's not a character flaw. It's a survival response. Your body is trying to protect you, even if the threat is long gone.

I remember sitting in a coffee shop once, wanting to move to a different table because the chair was uncomfortable and the light was too bright. And I couldn't do it. I sat there for forty-five minutes, uncomfortable, because the thought of getting up and moving felt impossible. It felt dangerous. Like someone would see me, judge me, tell me I didn't deserve to be comfortable.

Wild, right? That's what a nervous system stuck in protection mode feels like. It doesn't matter that the rational part of your brain knows it's fine. The body doesn't care about rational. The body cares about survival.

So learning to take up space starts with the body. Not with affirmations. Not with positive thinking. Not with telling yourself you deserve to be here, though that can help eventually. It starts with noticing what happens in your body when you think about being seen. When you think about asking for something. When you think about taking up more room.

Notice what just happened in your body as you read that. Did your shoulders tighten? Did your stomach clench? Did your breath get shallow? That's your nervous system talking. And it's not wrong. It's just stuck in an old story.

The shame of wanting to be seen

Here's something nobody talks about. When you've been shrinking for years, the idea of taking up space doesn't just feel scary. It feels shameful. Like you're being selfish. Like you're doing something wrong. Like you're breaking some unspoken rule that says you should stay small, stay quiet, stay out of the way.

That shame is not yours. I mean that literally. It was given to you. By parents who couldn't handle your bigness. By teachers who punished your enthusiasm. By a culture that rewards compliance and punishes presence. By people who were threatened by your light because theirs had been dimmed.

Pete Walker, who wrote about complex PTSD, talks about the fawn response. It's a survival strategy where you learn to please others, to merge with their needs, to disappear into their expectations, because that's how you stay safe. And it works. Until it doesn't. Until you look around and realize you've built a life around being what everyone else needs and you have no idea what you actually want.

A gentle approach to working with shame and self-rejection is Radical Acceptance (paid link) by Tara Brach. It's not about fixing yourself. It's about learning to be with what's here without turning away. And that includes the shame. That includes the fear. That includes the part of you that's terrified of being seen.

Because here's the thing about shame. It thrives in secrecy. It grows in the dark. The moment you start talking about it, naming it, letting someone else see it, it starts to lose its power. Not all at once. But slowly. Like ice melting in a warm room.

I started telling my therapist about the shame I felt when I wanted something. When I wanted to speak up in a meeting. When I wanted to ask for a hug. When I wanted to sit in a different chair at a coffee shop. And just saying it out loud, having someone witness it without judgment, that was the beginning of something shifting.

Taking up space in small increments

You don't go from shrinking to taking up space overnight. That's not how nervous systems work. You do it in small, almost invisible increments. You practice in low-stakes environments where the cost of being seen is manageable.

Maybe that means moving to a different seat in a coffee shop. Maybe it means saying no to something you don't want to do. Maybe it means letting your voice get a little louder in a conversation. Maybe it means wearing a color that makes you feel visible.

These things sound small. They sound trivial. But to a nervous system that learned that visibility equals danger, they're not small at all. They're acts of rebellion. They're tiny revolutions against a lifetime of conditioning.

Deb Dana, who works extensively with Polyvagal Theory, talks about something called glimmers. These are small moments of safety and connection that your nervous system can learn to recognize and anchor into. They're the opposite of triggers. And they can be as simple as the warmth of sunlight on your skin, the sound of a friend's laugh, the feeling of your feet on the ground.

I started collecting glimmers. I kept a note on my phone where I wrote down moments when I felt safe enough to be a little more myself. When I said something honest and nobody punished me for it. When I asked for what I needed and got it. When I took up space and the world didn't end.

A practical guide to working with your nervous system is Anchored (paid link) by Deb Dana. It's full of small, concrete practices for helping your nervous system learn that it's safe to be here. To be seen. To take up space.

The key is to go slow. To not force it. To let your body lead the way. Because if you try to take up space before your nervous system is ready, you'll just retraumatize yourself. You'll push too hard, hit a wall, and then feel like a failure. That's not helpful. That's just more evidence for the part of you that says it's not safe to be seen.

So go slow. Really slow. Like, embarrassingly slow. Like, "I moved my chair six inches to the left and that was my win for the day" slow. That counts. That's real progress. That's your nervous system learning, one tiny increment at a time, that the world is not as dangerous as it once was.

What happens when you start taking up space

Here's something nobody warns you about. When you start taking up space, some people will not like it. They will not like the new you. They will prefer the old you, the one who was small and quiet and easy to be around. The one who didn't ask for things. The one who didn't have opinions. The one who made them feel comfortable by being uncomfortable.

And that will hurt. It will hurt a lot. Because some of those people are people you love. People you've been shrinking for. People who maybe didn't even know they were asking you to be small, but they were. And now that you're not small anymore, they don't know what to do with you.

That's not your fault. That's not a sign that you're doing something wrong. That's a sign that you're changing, and change is uncomfortable for everyone involved. But you don't have to go back to being small just to make other people comfortable. You don't have to set yourself on fire to keep other people warm.

A compassionate reframing of trauma that resonates with many is What Happened to You? (paid link) by Bruce Perry and Oprah Winfrey. It's a reminder that the question isn't "what's wrong with you" but "what happened to you." And what happened to you is that you learned to shrink. And now you're learning something else.

I lost some relationships when I started taking up space. People who were used to me being a certain way, a certain size, a certain level of accommodating. And it hurt. It still hurts sometimes. But I also found new relationships. People who liked me big. People who weren't threatened by my presence. People who wanted me to take up space because they knew that's what it means to be alive.

And that's the thing. Taking up space is not selfish. It's not wrong. It's not something you have to earn or deserve. It's what it means to be human. It's what it means to be here, in this body, in this life, with all your wants and needs and desires and opinions and feelings.

You were born taking up space. You came into this world screaming, arms and legs flailing, demanding to be fed and held and seen. That's who you are underneath all the conditioning. That's who you're coming back to.

And yes, it's hard. It's scary. It's uncomfortable. You will want to go back to shrinking because shrinking is familiar and familiar feels safe even when it's killing you. But you don't have to. You can stay here, in the discomfort, and let your nervous system slowly, slowly learn that it's okay to be seen.

You can take up space. You can take up as much space as you need. The world is big enough for you. It always was.

Healing Journey Resources

  • What Happened to You? (paid link) - Bruce Perry and Oprah Winfrey
  • Anchored (paid link) - Deb Dana
  • Radical Acceptance (paid link) - Tara Brach
  • No Bad Parts (paid link) - Richard Schwartz
